food_subgroup: Bread and bread products with spread

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for peanut-spread soft French breadstick. FOODON_03302397 (bread product) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A000E (Bread and similar products) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for peanut-topped soft French breadstick.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226559009 (Bread) used as closest parent; no specific code for peanut soft French breadstick exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is one soft French breadstick (approximately 80 g) spread with peanut paste, as commonly served at Singaporean kopitiam chains such as Toast Box.

nutrient_source.title: Bread, French or Vienna, toasted (USDA SR Legacy #18033) and Peanut butter, smooth style (USDA SR Legacy #16098) — composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for peanut soft French breadstick exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA Branded Foods. Values estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for French/Vienna bread (~70% by weight of serving) and smooth peanut butter (~30% by weight of serving), proportioned to 100 g composite. This is a standard component-based estimation approach. Energy cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central entries for comparable peanut toast products.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; white bread and peanut butter composite analogue
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for peanut soft French breadstick. GI estimated at 72 based on the GI of white French baguette (GI ~72, University of Sydney GI Database) as the dominant carbohydrate contributor (~70% of serving weight). Peanut butter has a low GI (~14) and its addition modestly lowers the composite GI; however, given the bread-dominant composition, the overall GI is estimated at 72 (high). Glycemic load per 80 g serving = (72 × 39.6 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 28.5, rounded to 28.4. Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283 used as peer-reviewed reference for GI values.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~320 mg per 100 g; ~256 mg per 80 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit consumption to one breadstick per occasion and avoid pairing with other high-sodium foods, as the sodium from bread and peanut paste can contribute meaningfully toward the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~72) and high glycemic load per serving (~28.4) driven by refined white French bread base
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this food sparingly or substitute with a wholegrain bread variant, as the high GI and GL of the refined French bread base can cause rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation; pairing with a protein or fibre source may partially attenuate the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~2.9 g per 100 g) and sodium; peanut butter provides beneficial monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fats
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the peanut paste component provides heart-beneficial unsaturated fats, but the refined bread base, moderate sodium, and small trans fat content warrant portion control; choosing a version made with natural peanut butter and limiting to one serving is advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Peanut Soft French Breadstick is a popular Singaporean kopitiam breakfast and snack item consisting of a soft, lightly baked French baguette-style bread roll or stick generously spread with sweetened peanut paste or peanut butter. It is a signature menu item at chains such as Toast Box and is commonly served alongside kopi (local coffee) or teh (local tea). The bread is characteristically soft and slightly chewy on the inside with a thin, lightly crisp crust, distinguishing it from the crunchier traditional French baguette. The peanut spread is typically sweetened and may contain added sugar, salt, and sometimes butter or margarine, giving it a rich, creamy, and mildly sweet flavour profile. Nutritionally, the item is energy-dense (~370 kcal per 100 g), with a moderate protein content (~10.8 g) from both the bread and peanut paste, a meaningful fat contribution (~14.2 g) dominated by heart-beneficial unsaturated fats from peanuts, and a high carbohydrate load (~49.5 g) from the refined white bread base. The glycemic index is high (~72), making it a concern for blood glucose management in individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium content is moderate (~320 mg per 100 g), relevant for hypertension management. The dish is a culturally embedded comfort food in Singapore's hawker and kopitiam culture, representing the local adaptation of Western bread formats into the Southeast Asian breakfast tradition.
common_user_queries: What is a peanut soft French breadstick?
How many calories in Toast Box peanut breadstick?
Is peanut soft French breadstick suitable for diabetics?
What is the sodium content of peanut breadstick?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for peanut soft French breadstick was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or any primary regional database. Nutrient values were estimated by component-based calculation using USDA SR Legacy data for French/Vienna bread and smooth peanut butter in approximate serving proportions. GI was estimated analogously from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white French baguette. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality approved sources and the estimation methodology is standard and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-based estimates derived from USDA SR Legacy entries for French/Vienna bread (USDA #18033) and smooth peanut butter (USDA #16098), proportioned approximately 70:30 by weight to reflect a typical peanut-spread breadstick. These values should be replaced with direct laboratory analysis or an exact HPB Singapore Foodo entry if one becomes available. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reliably reported in the composite source calculation and varies with butter/margarine type used. 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as this is a plant-dominant product and B12 is not meaningfully present; not reported in the USDA SR Legacy bread or peanut butter entries. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entries for bread or peanut butter. 5. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; neither French bread nor peanut butter contains meaningful vitamin C per USDA SR Legacy data. 6. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on white French baguette GI from the University of Sydney GI Database; the peanut butter component (GI ~14) has a minor moderating effect but the bread-dominant composition keeps the overall GI in the high range. 7. Halal status is assumed based on Toast Box's general halal certification in Singapore; kosher status is not confirmed. 8. Trans fat is estimated at 0.1 g per 100 g reflecting trace amounts from commercial margarine/shortening potentially used in bread production; this should be verified against the specific product formulation.
